1	// Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.

3	// SPDX-License-Identifier: LicenseRef-Proprietary
4	
5	//! Gallops type definitions
6	//!
7	//! Core data structures for Job Jockey heat and pace tracking.
8	
9	use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
10	use std::collections::BTreeMap;
11	
12	/// Unknown/default basis SHA (7 zeros)
13	///
14	/// Used when basis commit cannot be determined (git errors).
15	/// All basis-related code derives the expected length from this constant.
16	pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_BASIS: &str = "0000000";
17	
18	/// Pace state values
19	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
20	#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]
21	pub enum jjrg_PaceState {
22	    Rough,
23	    #[serde(alias = "primed")]
24	    Bridled,
25	    Complete,
26	    Abandoned,
27	}
28	
29	/// Heat status values
30	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
31	#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]
32	pub enum jjrg_HeatStatus {
33	    /// Heat is actively being worked
34	    Racing,
35	    /// Heat is paused, not actively worked
36	    Stabled,
37	    /// Heat is complete and archived (terminal state)
38	    Retired,
39	}
40	
41	/// Tack record - snapshot of Pace state and plan
42	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
43	pub struct jjrg_Tack {
44	    pub ts: String,
45	    pub state: jjrg_PaceState,
46	    pub text: String,
47	    pub silks: String,
48	    #[serde(rename = "commit")]
49	    pub basis: String,
50	    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
51	    pub direction: Option<String>,
52	}
53	
54	/// Pace record - discrete action within a Heat
55	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
56	pub struct jjrg_Pace {
57	    pub tacks: Vec<jjrg_Tack>,
58	}
59	
60	/// Initial pensum seed value (2-char base64url, starts at "AA")
61	pub const JJRT_PENSUM_SEED_INIT: &str = "AA";
62	
63	/// Heat record - bounded initiative
64	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
65	pub struct jjrg_Heat {
66	    pub silks: String,
67	    pub creation_time: String,
68	    pub status: jjrg_HeatStatus,
69	    pub order: Vec<String>,
70	    pub next_pace_seed: String,
71	    pub paces: BTreeMap<String, jjrg_Pace>,
72	}
73	
74	/// Root Gallops structure
75	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
76	pub struct jjrg_Gallops {
77	    #[serde(default)]
78	    pub schema_version: Option<u32>,
79	    pub next_heat_seed: String,
80	    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]
81	    pub heat_order: Vec<String>,
82	    pub heats: BTreeMap<String, jjrg_Heat>,
83	}
84	
85	/// Arguments for the nominate operation
86	pub struct jjrg_NominateArgs {
87	    pub silks: String,
88	    pub created: String,
89	}
90	
91	/// Result of the nominate operation
92	#[derive(Debug)]
93	pub struct jjrg_NominateResult {
94	    pub firemark: String,
95	}
96	
97	/// Arguments for the slate operation
98	pub struct jjrg_SlateArgs {
99	    pub firemark: String,
100	    pub silks: String,
101	    pub text: String,
102	    /// Coronet to insert before (mutually exclusive with after/first)
103	    pub before: Option<String>,
104	    /// Coronet to insert after (mutually exclusive with before/first)
105	    pub after: Option<String>,
106	    /// Insert at beginning (mutually exclusive with before/after)
107	    pub first: bool,
108	}
109	
110	/// Result of the slate operation
111	#[derive(Debug)]
112	pub struct jjrg_SlateResult {
113	    pub coronet: String,
114	}
115	
116	/// Arguments for the rail operation
117	///
118	/// Supports two modes:
119	/// - Order mode: provide `order` array to replace entire sequence
120	/// - Move mode: provide `move_coronet` + one positioning field to relocate a single pace
121	pub struct jjrg_RailArgs {
122	    pub firemark: String,
123	    /// Order mode: new sequence of all coronets
124	    pub order: Vec<String>,
125	    /// Move mode: coronet to relocate
126	    pub move_coronet: Option<String>,
127	    /// Move before this coronet
128	    pub before: Option<String>,
129	    /// Move after this coronet
130	    pub after: Option<String>,
131	    /// Move to beginning
132	    pub first: bool,
133	    /// Move to end
134	    pub last: bool,
135	}
136	
137	/// Arguments for the tally operation
138	pub struct jjrg_TallyArgs {
139	    pub coronet: String,
140	    pub state: Option<jjrg_PaceState>,
141	    pub direction: Option<String>,
142	    pub text: Option<String>,
143	    pub silks: Option<String>,
144	}
145	
146	/// Arguments for the draft operation
147	pub struct jjrg_DraftArgs {
148	    /// Coronet of the pace to move
149	    pub coronet: String,
150	    /// Destination heat Firemark
151	    pub to: String,
152	    /// Coronet to insert before (mutually exclusive with after/first)
153	    pub before: Option<String>,
154	    /// Coronet to insert after (mutually exclusive with before/first)
155	    pub after: Option<String>,
156	    /// Insert at beginning (mutually exclusive with before/after)
157	    pub first: bool,
158	}
159	
160	/// Result of the draft operation
161	#[derive(Debug)]
162	pub struct jjrg_DraftResult {
163	    /// New coronet in destination heat
164	    pub new_coronet: String,
165	}
166	
167	/// Arguments for the retire operation
168	pub struct jjrg_RetireArgs {
169	    /// Firemark of heat to retire
170	    pub firemark: String,
171	    /// Today's date in YYMMDD format (for trophy filename)
172	    pub today: String,
173	}
174	
175	/// Result of the retire operation
176	#[derive(Debug)]
177	pub struct jjrg_RetireResult {
178	    /// Path to created trophy file
179	    pub trophy_path: String,
180	    /// Path to deleted paddock file
181	    pub paddock_path: String,
182	    /// Heat silks (for commit message)
183	    pub silks: String,
184	    /// Firemark display string (for commit message)
185	    pub firemark: String,
186	}
187	
188	/// Arguments for the furlough operation
189	pub struct jjrg_FurloughArgs {
190	    /// Firemark of heat to furlough
191	    pub firemark: String,
192	    /// Set status to racing (mutually exclusive with stabled)
193	    pub racing: bool,
194	    /// Set status to stabled (mutually exclusive with racing)
195	    pub stabled: bool,
196	    /// New silks (rename heat)
197	    pub silks: Option<String>,
198	}
199	
200	/// Arguments for the restring operation
201	pub struct jjrg_RestringArgs {
202	    /// Source heat Firemark
203	    pub source_firemark: String,
204	    /// Destination heat Firemark
205	    pub dest_firemark: String,
206	    /// Coronets to transfer (in order)
207	    pub coronets: Vec<String>,
208	}
209	
210	/// Mapping of old coronet to new coronet with pace metadata
211	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
212	pub struct jjrg_RestringMapping {
213	    pub old_coronet: String,
214	    pub new_coronet: String,
215	    pub silks: String,
216	    pub state: jjrg_PaceState,
217	    pub spec: String,
218	}
219	
220	/// Result of the restring operation
221	#[derive(Debug)]
222	pub struct jjrg_RestringResult {
223	    /// Source heat info
224	    pub source_firemark: String,
225	    pub source_silks: String,
226	    pub source_paddock: String,
227	    pub source_empty_after: bool,
228	    /// Destination heat info
229	    pub dest_firemark: String,
230	    pub dest_silks: String,
231	    pub dest_paddock: String,
232	    /// Drafted pace mappings (in transfer order)
233	    pub drafted: Vec<jjrg_RestringMapping>,
234	}
235	
236	/// Context returned by resolve_pace — snapshot of current pace state
237	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
238	pub struct jjrg_PaceContext {
239	    /// Coronet display key
240	    pub coronet_key: String,
241	    /// Parent firemark display key
242	    pub firemark_key: String,
243	    /// Current pace state
244	    pub state: jjrg_PaceState,
245	    /// Current docket text
246	    pub text: String,
247	    /// Current silks
248	    pub silks: String,
249	    /// Current direction (if bridled)
250	    pub direction: Option<String>,
251	}
252

content.text:
I have enough to orient. Let me write up the inventory and frame the grooming.

## Heat ₣BE — `jjk-v4-2-sprue` orient

**Goal (as I read it):** Mint the unprefixed JSON tag vocabulary in `.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json` so wire-level names follow project prefix discipline. AXLA's sprue mechanism is the conceptual lever (MCM `mcm_sprue` = the literal wire token; quoin = the concept), but the precise minting policy is undefined.

**Current state:** Heat is empty (no paces, no paddock content).

### JSON tag inventory — `jjg_gallops.json` (one file, 9509 lines)

Sixteen distinct tags across four nesting levels (excludes the firemark/coronet keys, which already have minting):

| Tag | Rust field | Carrier struct | Notes |
|-----|------------|----------------|-------|
| `schema_version` | `schema_version` | `jjrg_Gallops` | bare name |
| `next_heat_seed` | `next_heat_seed` | `jjrg_Gallops` | bare |
| `heat_order` | `heat_order` | `jjrg_Gallops` | bare |
| `heats` | `heats` | `jjrg_Gallops` | bare |
| `silks` | `silks` | `jjrg_Heat`, `jjrg_Tack` | bare; reused across two structs |
| `creation_time` | `creation_time` | `jjrg_Heat` | bare |
| `status` | `status` | `jjrg_Heat` | bare; enum `jjrg_HeatStatus` |
| `order` | `order` | `jjrg_Heat` | bare |
| `next_pace_seed` | `next_pace_seed` | `jjrg_Heat` | bare |
| `paces` | `paces` | `jjrg_Heat` | bare |
| `tacks` | `tacks` | `jjrg_Pace` | bare |
| `ts` | `ts` | `jjrg_Tack` | bare |
| `state` | `state` | `jjrg_Tack` | bare; enum `jjrg_PaceState` |
| `text` | `text` | `jjrg_Tack` | bare |
| `commit` | `basis` (Rust) | `jjrg_Tack` | **already** has `#[serde(rename = "commit")]` — sole rename precedent |
| `direction` | `direction` | `jjrg_Tack` | bare, optional |

**Enum string values (also unprefixed wire tokens):**
- `state`: `rough`, `bridled` (alias `primed`), `complete`, `abandoned` (`jjrg_PaceState`)
- `status`: `racing`, `stabled`, `retired` (`jjrg_HeatStatus`)

### Do JSON tags have symbolic names in Rust?

**Partial.** The struct *types* are minted (`jjrg_Gallops`, `jjrg_Heat`, `jjrg_Pace`, `jjrg_Tack`, `jjrg_PaceState`, `jjrg_HeatStatus`), and `jjrg_PaceState::Bridled` carries `#[serde(alias = "primed")]` — so the *concept* identifiers exist. But there are no symbolic constants for the wire tags themselves — serde derives them from bare field names via implicit `to_lowercase` for enums. The only crack of light is `basis` field → `commit` JSON tag, the lone explicit rename. So in MCM/AXLA terms: **the quoins are minted, the sprues are not.**

### Co-located scope considerations

- `.claude/jjm/jjs_studbook.json` exists with a different unprefixed schema (`datestamp`, `display`, `id`, `paces[].id/display/status`, …) but `grep` shows no Rust source consumes it — appears stale. Worth confirming it's truly orphaned before this heat lands, otherwise the migration leaves it inconsistent.
- Gazette wire format already uses prefixed sprues (`jjezs_paddock`, `jjezs_slate`, `jjezs_reslate`) — that's the pattern the gallops migration would extend.
- MCP tool wire (`command`, `params`, `officium`, `model`) also unprefixed but is a Claude-facing wire — much bigger migration, presumably out of scope here.

### Open design questions before paces can slate

1. **Minting prefix.** `jjg_` (gallops-file scope) is the natural pick — matches the file basename and is currently terminal-free. Alternative: per-struct prefix (`jjg_h_silks` for heat, `jjg_t_silks` for tack) to disambiguate the silks-on-two-structs case. Recommend single `jjg_` because the same conceptual silks straddles both.
2. **Enum value scope.** Are `racing`/`stabled`/`rough`/`complete`/etc. in scope, or only object keys? The `bridled`/`primed` precedent suggests the project does treat these as wire tokens. If yes, `jjghs_racing`, `jjgps_rough`, etc.
3. **Are silks-values themselves wire names?** `jjk-v4-2-sprue` etc. — the user said "maybe some other unprefixed 'name' strings". Silks are by spec kebab-case display names not lookup keys, so likely out of scope, but worth nailing.
4. **Sprue protocol — the AXLA spec gap.** AXLA documents `mcm_sprue` as the wire token that *may* be an `mcm_inlay` if it follows prefix discipline. There is no documented protocol describing **how a JJK Rust struct field declares its sprue** (i.e., the convention layer that says "`#[serde(rename = ...)]` is the binding mechanism, the prefix is `jjg_`, and field-name-equals-sprue is forbidden after migration"). This is the "precise definition" gap you flagged.
5. **Migration mechanics.** One-file rewrite via a tool that loads-rewrites-saves with old→new key mapping, plus serde renames in `jjrt_types.rs`. The studbook & test fixtures need a sweep for the same tags.
6. **Compat.** `#[serde(alias = "...")]` on each renamed field would let the kit read pre-migration files for one transition window. Symmetric to how `bridled`/`primed` already coexists.

### Suggested pace breakdown (for your review — not slated)

- **BEAA** — *spec-the-sprue-protocol* — Add a section to AXLA (or a new JJK-side spec doc) stating: every JSON-serialized field in `jjrt_types.rs` is a sprue; sprue-name = `<prefix>_<concept>`; binding is `#[serde(rename = "...")]`; field-name-equals-sprue is banned. Pick `jjg_` or alternative. Decide enum-value scope. Decide compat-alias policy.
- **BEAB** — *mint-the-tags* — Apply `#[serde(rename)]` to every field/variant per the spec; rewrite `jjg_gallops.json` once; sweep `jjs_studbook.json` and any test fixtures; verify round-trip via `jjx_validate` and the test suite.
- **BEAC** (optional) — *retire-or-mint-studbook* — Either delete `jjs_studbook.json` if confirmed dead, or apply the same minting if it's load-bearing somewhere I didn't grep.

Want me to draft dockets for these, or reshape the breakdown first? My main uncertainty is OQ-4: whether the precise sprue protocol belongs in AXLA itself (cross-project concern) or in a JJK-internal spec.

1	# Job Jockey Kit
2	
3	## What is Job Jockey?
4	
5	Job Jockey (JJ) is a lightweight system for managing project initiatives through conversation with Claude Code. It helps you track bounded heats, remember what's next, and keep a backlog of ideas without drowning in ceremony or context bloat.
6	
7	Think of it as a project notebook specifically designed for human-AI collaboration:
8	- **Heats** are your current work (3-50 chat sessions worth)
9	- **Paces** track what's done and what's next within a heat
10	- **Itches** capture future ideas without losing focus
11	- **Scars** record closed ideas with lessons learned
12	
13	The system is ephemeral by design: documents have clear lifecycles, completed work gets archived, and context stays lean. Everything is markdown, lives in git, and can move between computers with you.
14	
15	## Naming Prefixes
16	
17	All Job Jockey artifacts use the `jj` prefix with category-specific third letters:
18	
19	| Prefix | Category | Purpose |
20	|--------|----------|---------|
21	| `jja_` | Action | Slash commands |
22	| `jjb_` | Brand | Current detected version |
23	| `jjc_` | Chase | Steeplechase performance logs |
24	| `jjg_` | aGent | (Future) Agent definitions |
25	| `jjh_` | Heat | Bounded initiative files |
26	| `jji_` | Itch | Future work aggregate |
27	| `jjk_` | sKill | (Future) Skill definitions |
28	| `jjl_` | Ledger | Version history registry |
29	| `jjm/` | Memory | State directory (`.claude/jjm/`) |
30	| `jjn_` | Notch | (Future) JJ-aware git commits |
31	| `jjs_` | Scar | Closed work aggregate |
32	
33	## Core Concepts
34	
35	### Heat
36	A bounded initiative with **coherent goals that are clear and present**. Spans 3-50 chat sessions. Has a goal, context section, and list of paces. Lives as a dated file like `jjh_b251108-buk-portability.md`.
37	
38	Heat location indicates state:
39	- `current/` — actively working
40	- `retired/` — completed (retire date added to filename: `jjh_b251108-r251126-buk-portability.md`)
41	
42	A heat must be timely. If work is well-specified but not timely, it remains an itch until the time is right.
43	
44	### Pace
45	A discrete action within the current heat. Appears in heat documents as structured sections. Pending paces can have detailed descriptions. Completed paces get condensed to brief summaries to save context.
46	
47	Paces can be worked on collaboratively (human drives, model assists) or **armed** for autonomous execution. To arm a pace, use `/jja-pace-arm` which validates the spec has clear objective, bounded scope, success criteria, and failure behavior. Armed paces can then be flown with `/jja-pace-fly`.
48	
49	### Itch
50	Future work of any scope — heat-sized initiatives, individual paces, or smaller ideas — captured in the backlog because they're not timely for current work. The key attribute is **not now**: regardless of size or detail level, it's not ready to schedule. All itches live in a single aggregate backlog file (`jji_itch.md`).
51	
52	### Scar
53	An itch that has been **closed with lessons learned**. Not deleted (we learned something), but won't be revisited. Different from "shelved" which implies "maybe later" — a scar is deliberately closed.
54	
55	All scars live in a single aggregate file (`jjs_scar.md`).
56	
57	### Silks
58	The kebab-case identifier that names heats, paces, itches, and scars. Examples: `cloud-first-light`, `fix-quota-bug`, `buc-info-default-visibility`. Target 3-5 words — short enough to say aloud and fit in commit messages. Good silks are memorable and evocative.
59	
60	## How It Works
61	
62	### Day-to-Day Usage
63	
64	You work on a heat by talking with Claude Code. All commands route through the `mcp__vvx__jjx` MCP tool. As you make progress:
65	- At session start, say "mount" to establish context and see proposed approach
66	- Work on the pace together
67	- Say "wrap" to mark complete, then mount the next pace
68	- Say "notch" mid-pace when you want to commit progress without wrapping
69	- New paces emerge via "slate"
70	
71	When new ideas come up that don't belong in current heat, add them to `jji_itch.md`.
72	
73	When a heat completes, say "retire" to move it to `retired/` with a datestamp.
74	
75	### Interaction Pattern
76	
77	The system is **conversational and collaborative**:
78	- Claude proposes actions ("I'll mark this pace done and summarize it as...")
79	- You approve or amend ("yes" / "change it to..." / "no, actually...")
80	- Changes commit to git automatically after approval
81	- You maintain control, Claude does the bookkeeping
82	
83	### Context Management
84	
85	The system is designed to minimize context usage:
86	- Completed paces become one-line summaries
87	- Only current heat is in regular context
88	- Itches and scars stay out of context unless needed
89	- Full history preserved in git, not in active documents
90	
91	## File Structure
92	
93	### `jjh_bYYMMDD-description.md` (Job Jockey Heat)
94	Main context document for a heat.
95	- **Active**: Named with begin date and description (e.g., `jjh_b251108-buk-portability.md`)
96	- **Retired**: Begin date preserved, retire date added (e.g., `jjh_b251108-r251126-buk-portability.md`)
97	- Located in: `.claude/jjm/current/` (active) or `.claude/jjm/retired/` (completed)
98	
99	### `jji_itch.md` (Job Jockey Itches)
100	**All** itches live here — the single source of future work.

# Job Jockey Kit

## What is Job Jockey?

Job Jockey (JJ) is a lightweight system for managing project initiatives through conversation with Claude Code. It helps you track bounded heats, remember what's next, and keep a backlog of ideas without drowning in ceremony or context bloat.

Think of it as a project notebook specifically designed for human-AI collaboration:
- **Heats** are your current work (3-50 chat sessions worth)
- **Paces** track what's done and what's next within a heat
- **Itches** capture future ideas without losing focus
- **Scars** record closed ideas with lessons learned

The system is ephemeral by design: documents have clear lifecycles, completed work gets archived, and context stays lean. Everything is markdown, lives in git, and can move between computers with you.

## Naming Prefixes

All Job Jockey artifacts use the `jj` prefix with category-specific third letters:

| Prefix | Category | Purpose |
|--------|----------|---------|
| `jja_` | Action | Slash commands |
| `jjb_` | Brand | Current detected version |
| `jjc_` | Chase | Steeplechase performance logs |
| `jjg_` | aGent | (Future) Agent definitions |
| `jjh_` | Heat | Bounded initiative files |
| `jji_` | Itch | Future work aggregate |
| `jjk_` | sKill | (Future) Skill definitions |
| `jjl_` | Ledger | Version history registry |
| `jjm/` | Memory | State directory (`.claude/jjm/`) |
| `jjn_` | Notch | (Future) JJ-aware git commits |
| `jjs_` | Scar | Closed work aggregate |

## Core Concepts

### Heat
A bounded initiative with **coherent goals that are clear and present**. Spans 3-50 chat sessions. Has a goal, context section, and list of paces. Lives as a dated file like `jjh_b251108-buk-portability.md`.

Heat location indicates state:
- `current/` — actively working
- `retired/` — completed (retire date added to filename: `jjh_b251108-r251126-buk-portability.md`)

A heat must be timely. If work is well-specified but not timely, it remains an itch until the time is right.

### Pace
A discrete action within the current heat. Appears in heat documents as structured sections. Pending paces can have detailed descriptions. Completed paces get condensed to brief summaries to save context.

Paces can be worked on collaboratively (human drives, model assists) or **armed** for autonomous execution. To arm a pace, use `/jja-pace-arm` which validates the spec has clear objective, bounded scope, success criteria, and failure behavior. Armed paces can then be flown with `/jja-pace-fly`.

### Itch
Future work of any scope — heat-sized initiatives, individual paces, or smaller ideas — captured in the backlog because they're not timely for current work. The key attribute is **not now**: regardless of size or detail level, it's not ready to schedule. All itches live in a single aggregate backlog file (`jji_itch.md`).

### Scar
An itch that has been **closed with lessons learned**. Not deleted (we learned something), but won't be revisited. Different from "shelved" which implies "maybe later" — a scar is deliberately closed.

All scars live in a single aggregate file (`jjs_scar.md`).

### Silks
The kebab-case identifier that names heats, paces, itches, and scars. Examples: `cloud-first-light`, `fix-quota-bug`, `buc-info-default-visibility`. Target 3-5 words — short enough to say aloud and fit in commit messages. Good silks are memorable and evocative.

## How It Works

### Day-to-Day Usage

You work on a heat by talking with Claude Code. All commands route through the `mcp__vvx__jjx` MCP tool. As you make progress:
- At session start, say "mount" to establish context and see proposed approach
- Work on the pace together
- Say "wrap" to mark complete, then mount the next pace
- Say "notch" mid-pace when you want to commit progress without wrapping
- New paces emerge via "slate"

When new ideas come up that don't belong in current heat, add them to `jji_itch.md`.

When a heat completes, say "retire" to move it to `retired/` with a datestamp.

### Interaction Pattern

The system is **conversational and collaborative**:
- Claude proposes actions ("I'll mark this pace done and summarize it as...")
- You approve or amend ("yes" / "change it to..." / "no, actually...")
- Changes commit to git automatically after approval
- You maintain control, Claude does the bookkeeping

### Context Management

The system is designed to minimize context usage:
- Completed paces become one-line summaries
- Only current heat is in regular context
- Itches and scars stay out of context unless needed
- Full history preserved in git, not in active documents

## File Structure

### `jjh_bYYMMDD-description.md` (Job Jockey Heat)
Main context document for a heat.
- **Active**: Named with begin date and description (e.g., `jjh_b251108-buk-portability.md`)
- **Retired**: Begin date preserved, retire date added (e.g., `jjh_b251108-r251126-buk-portability.md`)
- Located in: `.claude/jjm/current/` (active) or `.claude/jjm/retired/` (completed)

### `jji_itch.md` (Job Jockey Itches)
**All** itches live here — the single source of future work.

content.text:
README updated. Now on `jjgt_` and the dry run.

## Concerns with `jjgt_<term>` for sprues

Mostly I think this is good. The X=g (Gallops domain), Y=t (Tag) decomposition cleanly fits the `prefixXY_word` shape from the Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline, and the `jjg_gallops.json` filename re-reads naturally as "the file at the root of the Gallops domain." Specific concerns:

1. **`silks` shadowing across structs.** `silks` lives on both `jjrg_Heat` and `jjrg_Tack`. Minting both as `jjgt_silks` collapses them to one sprue, which is a soft commitment that the *concept* is shared (display name, kebab-case). If you ever want to specialize one, you'd need a new sprue. I think that's right — they are conceptually parallel — but worth noting.
2. **`order` vs `heat_order` asymmetry.** `jjrg_Gallops::heat_order` and `jjrg_Heat::order` (for paces) are conceptually parallel but spelled differently today. Two clean futures: (a) keep the asymmetry — `jjgt_heat_order` and `jjgt_order` — or (b) introduce `jjgt_pace_order` for symmetry, requiring a Rust field rename. (b) is nicer; (a) is smaller.
3. **The pre-existing `basis` → `commit` rename.** `jjrg_Tack::basis` currently serializes to `"commit"`. Under minting, options are `jjgt_basis` (match the Rust field, retire the historical wire-name anomaly) or `jjgt_commit` (preserve the current wire name). I'd pick `jjgt_basis` — the legacy `commit` wire name was a stand-in and minting is the right moment to retire it.
4. **Enum string values are a separate question.** `"rough"`, `"bridled"`, `"racing"`, etc. are wire tokens too, but they sit on the RHS of `"state": ...` rather than being keys. They need their own scheme — `jjgt_rough`? `jjgs_rough` (s for State-value)? `jjghs_racing` (Heat Status)? This is a substantive design call that deserves to be settled in the spec-pace, not the dry run. Probably want to **scope the dry run to keys only** and leave enum values for the spec-pace to nail down.
5. **AXLA "sprue ≠ inlay" upgrade.** MCM says sprues need *no* prefix discipline by default; one that follows discipline becomes an *inlay*. `jjgt_*` makes our sprues into inlays — a stronger commitment. The spec-pace should state this explicitly (one paragraph in JJS0 / AXLA citing MCM lines 998-1002).
6. **Studbook collateral.** `jjs_studbook.json` has its own bare schema (`datestamp`, `display`, `id`, `paces[]`, …) and appears unconsumed by Rust. Either confirm-and-delete in this heat or leave it explicitly out-of-scope. Otherwise the dry-run "we mint sprues across the kit" claim is partially false.
7. **MCP wire format is a different beast.** `command`, `params`, `officium`, `model` on the MCP tool are also bare wire tokens but they straddle a Claude/server boundary, so much heavier migration. The spec-pace should explicitly mark these out-of-scope so we don't accidentally accrete the work.

## Dry-run proposal

Pick **one** Rust struct field — I'd pick `silks` — and run it end-to-end through every layer so we shake out the migration mechanism before scaling up:

| Layer | Action |
|-------|--------|
| Rust types | `#[serde(rename = "jjgt_silks", alias = "silks")]` on `jjrg_Heat::silks` and `jjrg_Tack::silks`. The `alias` lets us read pre-migration files; the `rename` makes us emit the new tag. |
| Spec | Add a "Sprue Vocabulary" section to JJS0 listing `jjgt_silks` with its meaning, plus the AXLA voicing pattern (one annotation worked example) |
| On-disk JSON | Run a one-shot rewriter against `jjg_gallops.json` mapping `"silks":` → `"jjgt_silks":` (also any test fixtures that mention silks in literal JSON) |
| Validation | Round-trip via existing tests + `jjx_validate`; confirm a fresh `jjx_record` writes the new tag |

That validates: (a) serde rename+alias mechanics, (b) the spec-pattern reads naturally for one sprue, (c) the on-disk rewrite path, (d) the test suite still passes. After that the bulk pace can fan out the same recipe to the remaining 14 keys.

Sound right? If so, the heat shapes up as three paces:

- **BEAA — spec the sprue protocol.** Define `jjgt_*` as the gallops-key sprue prefix, AXLA voicing pattern, decision on enum-values (probably `jjgs_*` for State, `jjghs_*` for HeatStatus — but TBD in the pace), `basis`/`commit` resolution, compat-alias policy, studbook scope.
- **BEAB — dry-run on `silks`.** End-to-end one-tag migration per the table above.
- **BEAC — fan out.** Apply the validated recipe to the remaining 14 keys + enum values; rewrite gallops.json once; sweep test fixtures.

content.text:
Two concerns with the literal `jjght_/jjgpt_` shape, plus a clean variant of the same idea:

### Concern 1 — 3-letter sub-prefix violates the discipline ceiling

`jjght_silks` decomposes as `jj` + `g` + `h` + `t` + `_silks` — three sub-letters (g=Gallops, h=Heat, t=Tag). The Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline in CLAUDE.md says **hard 2-letter ceiling** because the RBS0/JJS0 study found 3-letter sub-prefixes "proven opaque." This proposal re-introduces exactly that anti-pattern.

### Concern 2 — Y-monosemy collapse

The discipline says each sub-letter position has one meaning per domain. In `jjght_*`, position-1 means "domain" (Gallops), position-2 means "table" (Heat), position-3 means "type" (Tag). Three semantic axes packed into three letters — readers can't tell which letter carries which dimension without consulting a legend.

### Cleaner version of the same idea — drop the Tag letter

If table-discrimination is the real goal, let table-letter *be* Y. Two-letter sub-prefix preserved, full uniqueness, discipline intact:

| Sub-prefix | Carrier | Tags it covers |
|------------|---------|---------------|
| `jjgg_*` | Gallops root (`jjrg_Gallops`) | `jjgg_schema_version`, `jjgg_next_heat_seed`, `jjgg_heat_order`, `jjgg_heats` |
| `jjgh_*` | Heat (`jjrg_Heat`) | `jjgh_silks`, `jjgh_creation_time`, `jjgh_status`, `jjgh_order`, `jjgh_next_pace_seed`, `jjgh_paces` |
| `jjgp_*` | Pace (`jjrg_Pace`) | `jjgp_tacks` |
| `jjgt_*` | Tack (`jjrg_Tack`) | `jjgt_ts`, `jjgt_state`, `jjgt_text`, `jjgt_silks`, `jjgt_basis`, `jjgt_direction` |

17 unique sprues, no shadowing, table-letter is the only Y-axis. The "Tag" semantics is implicit — everything under `jjg{X}_` in the gallops file is a wire tag by construction.

Two side-notes on this variant:
- **`jjgg_*` is a mild stutter.** Aesthetic, not a hazard. Real risk: typo to `jjg_`.
- **`jjgp_*` is letter-adjacent to `jjp_*`** (paddock-file basename pattern like `jjp_uAl_.md`). Different namespace — no hard conflict — but a reader scanning quickly might confuse them. Worth a one-line callout in JJS0.

### The deeper question — is global uniqueness worth the cost?

Of the 16 tags, only **three** genuinely shadow:
- `silks` on Heat + Tack (parallel display names — arguably the same concept)
- `state` on Tack only / `status` on Heat only (different words; not actually shadowed)
- `order` on Heat / `heat_order` on Gallops (already disambiguated in current spelling)

Forcing per-table prefixes encodes the table dimension into every wire token, even the 13 that have no ambiguity. Counter-argument: a uniform rule is easier to author and read than "table-prefix only when shadowed." Reasonable people land either way.

**My weak read:**
- If global uniqueness matters to you → go with the table-letter variant (`jjgg_/jjgh_/jjgp_/jjgt_`). Drop the `t`-for-Tag letter.
- If shadowing-where-conceptually-shared feels right → stick with the original `jjgt_<term>` and accept that `jjgt_silks` reads as "the silks sprue, wherever it appears."
- The literal `jjght_/jjgpt_` shape (3-letter) is the worst of both worlds — uniqueness *and* discipline cost.

Which axis matters more to you? Once you pick, the dry-run is mechanically the same (the recipe just substitutes the chosen prefix scheme).

On the term: **"sprue"** is the project-native answer. MCM already defines it precisely:

> A literal wire-level token in backtick delimiters representing a serialized name. … the exact character sequence as it appears in a wire format — a JSON property key, an API field name, a protocol identifier.

| `jjgps_*` | Pace | g + p (Pace) + s |
| `jjgts_*` | Tack | g + t (Tack) + s |

Alternatives I considered and discarded:
- **field** (`f`) — schema-side word, not wire-side; loses the AXLA connection
- **key** (`k`) — accurate but generic; doesn't carry MCM's deliberate distinction between *quoin* (concept) and *sprue* (wire token)
- **inlay** (`i`) — MCM defines an inlay as "a sprue that follows prefix discipline." So inlay describes a *property* of the minted sprue, not the role; using `i` would conflate property with role

`s` for sprue keeps the role letter open for future siblings (e.g., a future `jjghv_*` for validators, `jjghc_*` for comment annotations) and aligns wire-name minting with the kit's broader MCM/AXLA vocabulary.

### Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline (MCM Spec-Internal)

For MCM concept model specs, apply this discipline on top of the general minting rules:

See minting memo Pattern J (RBS0/JJS0 empirical study) for evidence.

### Minting Workflow

Before minting new prefixes:
1. **Enumerate namespaces** — list every place this name will appear (code, refs, commands, env vars, target paths...)
2. **Check reserved suffixes** — ensure the suffix matches intended type

message.content: I asked you a json naming question, not a project naming.  How does the 'json community' name these strings that are dictionary keys?

content.text:
Right — I overshot into project framing. JSON community vocabulary, in order of authoritativeness:

| Source | Term | Letter |
|--------|------|--------|
| **RFC 8259 / ECMA-404** (the JSON spec itself) | **name** — "An object is an unordered collection of zero or more name/value pairs"; object entries are "members" | `n` |
| **JSON Schema, OpenAPI, ECMAScript** | **property** — schemas declare a `properties` object; entries are "property names" | `p` |
| Colloquial / casual usage | **key** — as in "key-value pair" | `k` |

"Tag" isn't standard JSON vocabulary — it leaks in from XML/HTML and protocol-buffer worlds. The strict-spec answer is **"name"**; the most-used technical term across JSON-adjacent ecosystems (JSON Schema, OpenAPI, JavaScript) is **"property"**.

One mechanical wrinkle: `p`-for-property collides letter-wise with `p`-for-Pace in the table position, giving `jjgpp_tacks` — a stuttering double-p on the Pace row. `n` (name) and `k` (key) avoid that. So if "property" is the term you want conceptually, the prefix shape pays an aesthetic tax on one row; if you want clean letters, "name" or "key" buys it.

### Migration code is just serde aliases

The "add migration code → run → remove migration code" arc maps cleanly onto `#[serde(rename = ..., alias = ...)]`:

```rust

pub silks: String,

// During migration window
#[serde(rename = "jjghn_silks", alias = "silks")]
pub silks: String,

// After migration window (alias removed)
#[serde(rename = "jjghn_silks")]
pub silks: String,
```

Same shape for PaceState variants. **No separate one-shot binary needed** — the "migration code" is literally the `alias` line. The rewrite trigger is any state-mutating jjx command run during the alias window (your own `jjx_record` to commit the pace's code changes will do it naturally; the deserializer reads the old `silks`/`rough` via alias, the serializer writes `jjghn_silks`/`jjgte_rough` because that's the rename target).

1. Add `#[serde(rename = "jjghn_silks", alias = "silks")]` to `jjrg_Heat::silks` and parallel renames+aliases on the four PaceState variants. Keep the existing `alias = "primed"` on `Bridled` (legacy precedent).
2. `tt/vow-b.Build.sh` — confirm clean compile.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BE

# Paddock: jjk-v4-2-sprue

## Goal

Mint the unprefixed JSON property names and enum values in `.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json` so wire-level tokens follow the project's prefix discipline. The kit's `jjrt_types.rs` types are already prefixed (quoins, in MCM terms); this heat introduces the matching wire-side names (sprues — inlays once they follow discipline).

## Sprue scheme

Three sub-letters after `jj`:

- **X** (constant): `g` — Gallops domain
- **Y** (table-letter): the carrier struct in `jjrt_types.rs` — `r` for `jjrg_Gallops` (root), `h` for `jjrg_Heat`, `p` for `jjrg_Pace`, `t` for `jjrg_Tack`. For enum values, Y is the carrier of the field that holds the enum
- **Z** (role-letter): `n` for property name, `e` for enum value

The suffix after the underscore is the Rust field name (for properties) or the lowercase variant name (for enum values).

## Migration recipe

Per target, two edits:

1. Add `#[serde(rename = "<minted>", alias = "<old-wire-name>")]`. Build. Run any state-mutating jjx call to round-trip the gallops file through the new wire-names.
2. Remove the transitional `alias` line.

The blanket `#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]` comes off `jjrg_PaceState` and `jjrg_HeatStatus` once explicit per-variant renames replace it. The pre-existing `alias = "primed"` on `Bridled` stays as legacy compat — outside this heat's window.

## Batches and order

Carrier-isolated batches keep the silks doppelganger (Heat + Tack) and the three Gallops-vs-Heat near-doppelgangers (heats/paces, heat_order/order, next_heat_seed/next_pace_seed) in distinct paces:

1. **mint-heat-silks-pace-states** — `jjghn_silks` plus the four `jjrg_PaceState` variants. Proves the recipe end-to-end.
2. **mint-gallops-root-sprues** — the four properties on `jjrg_Gallops`.
3. **mint-heat-rest-and-status** — the five remaining `jjrg_Heat` properties plus the three `jjrg_HeatStatus` variants.
4. **mint-pace-and-tack-sprues** — `jjgpn_tacks` plus the six `jjrg_Tack` properties.

Subsequent dockets reference these by silks rather than re-listing targets.

## Spec touch

content.text:
Yes — the gallops file is fine, the error is a code-side type mismatch.

**What's happening:** The kit has two type definitions:
- `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/src/jjrt_types.rs` — current v4 schema, no `paddock_file` field on `jjrg_Heat`
- `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/src/jjrt_v3_types.rs` — frozen v3 schema reference, requires `paddock_file: String` on `jjrg_Heat`

The on-disk gallops file is v4 (`"schema_version": 4`, no `paddock_file` on any heat — confirmed by inspecting line 1338 which is the closing `}` of a heat object that ends without it). The other chat's binary is somehow deserializing with **v3 types against a v4 file**, so serde reports `paddock_file` missing at the position where the heat object closes.

**Why it's likely not their code change:** In committed source, nothing imports `jjrt_v3_types` outside the `pub mod jjrt_v3_types;` declaration in `lib.rs`. The frozen module's own comment says "*Reference for V3→V4 migration in jjdr_load*" — but `jjdr_load` doesn't exist in the tree. The v3 types are reference-only today; no active load path uses them.

**Most likely causes for the other officium:**
1. Their working tree has uncommitted local changes that wired v3 types into a load path. `git status` + `git diff` on `jjri_io.rs`, `jjro_ops.rs`, or any new `jjdr_*` file would surface it.
2. Or they're running a stale binary built from an earlier branch that had the migration in-flight.

**Diagnostic for the other chat:**
- `grep -rn "jjrt_v3_types::" Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/src/` — should return zero non-comment hits in clean source. Anything beyond the `pub mod` declaration in `lib.rs` is the problem.
- `git status` on the JJK source tree.
- Rebuild against current `main` (`tt/vow-b.Build.sh`).

The reported error message ("looks like corrupted gallops state") is misleading — the gallops file is structurally valid v4. The corruption is in the type they're parsing it with.
